overview: The infrastructure domain encompasses the foundational technical environment necessary to support the responsible, scalable, and inclusive use of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education. It includes physical and virtual computing resources (e.g. GPUs, servers), networking, cloud services, data storage, AI platforms and software tools, system integration, and long-term maintenance capabilities. This infrastructure supports all core institutional functions—teaching, learning, research, and administration. Its quality and accessibility directly influence the institution’s ability to host, develop, deploy, and sustain AI-driven solutions. Without robust and future-ready infrastructure, most aspects of digital transformation— including data analytics, smart services, or AI-supported teaching—cannot be effectively implemented or scaled. A mature infrastructure environment is a key enabler of institutional innovation, allowing for experimentation while ensuring long-term operational sustainability. This area is also closely aligned with broader European digital transformation priorities, including the Digital Decade targets and the evolving regulatory landscape for AI and data governance. Institutions with a strong infrastructure backbone are better positioned to participate in cross-border initiatives, adopt emerging technologies, and meet future demands for secure and ethical AI integration. EN Page 75 of 118 ALFA Labs 2024-1-FI01-KA220-HED-000248157 trAnsformation pLan KA152, 153, 154 – Learning Mobility of Individuals For Ai Living Labs Final Beneficiary Report

instructions.maturity_scoring: For each item/statement in the following table, select: [0] Not in place | [1] Partially in place | [2] Fully in place, providing also clear evidence for the suggested score and owner responsible for this item. Examples for evidence and owner are provided within the table below. Note that items which are marked as Critical (with red dot) need to be implemented before approaching to higher maturity level (necessary/minimum conditions).

items.item: Availability of AI-ready computing resources (e.g. GPUs, servers, high- performance clusters) Notes: Focus on local/hybrid physical infrastructure

score_anchors.0: No dedicated AI computing resources; only general-purpose or outdated hardware.
score_anchors.1: Some AI- ready resources available in select areas or for pilot use.
score_anchors.2: Comprehensive, scalable AI-ready resources available institution-wide.

items.evidence_examples: Inventory of hardware, access logs, purchase records, user feedback

items.item: Secure access to cloud-based AI platforms (for training, experimentation, deployment) Notes: Covers cloud solutions and sandbox environments

score_anchors.0: No access or only ad hoc/individual cloud use.
score_anchors.1: Limited or pilot access to cloud AI platforms; partial security controls.
score_anchors.2: Secure, institution-wide access to cloud AI platforms with managed accounts and sandboxing.

items.evidence_examples: Cloud service nagreements,access logs,security audit reports
items.owners: Director of IT / Digital Services, External Providers (Cloud vendors, hosting partners)

items.item: Integration of AI tools with institutional systems (e.g. LMS, SIS, research platforms) Notes: Could split LMS/SIS if needed

score_anchors.0: No integration; AI tools used in isolation.
score_anchors.1: Partial or pilot integration with some core systems.
score_anchors.2: Full integration of AI tools with all major institutional systems.

items.evidence_examples: Integration documentation, system logs, user surveys
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services, AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

items.item: Existence of a dedicated and sustainable budget for AI/IT infrastructure Notes: Emphasizes long-term planning, not one-off purchases

score_anchors.0: No dedicated budget; only ad hoc or project- based funding.
score_anchors.1: Budget line exists but is limited or unstable.
score_anchors.2: Multi-year, sustainable budget with clear ownership and planning.

items.evidence_examples: Budget documents, multi-year plans, finance reports
items.owners: Chief Information Officer (CIO) or secretary/treasury managers

items.item: IT personnel trained to maintain and support AI- specific systems and AI-specific infrastructure Notes: Ensures human capacity and operational stability

score_anchors.0: No staff with AI infrastructure skills.
score_anchors.1: Some staff trained or upskilling in progress.
score_anchors.2: Sufficient, trained staff supporting AI infrastructure institution-wide.

items.evidence_examples: Training records, HR reports, support ticket logs
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

items.item: Inclusive and equitable access to infrastructure (for students and staff) Notes: Focus on accessibility, location, time, bandwidth, etc.

score_anchors.0: Access is inconsistent, limited by location/time, or not inclusive.
score_anchors.1: Partial improvements; some policies or pilots for accessibility.
score_anchors.2: Full, equitable access for all users, with policies and monitoring in place.

items.evidence_examples: Access policies, usage analytics, accessibility audits
items.owners: Accessibility and Inclusion Officer, Faculty and Department Heads, Student and Staff Representatives

items.item: Data architecture and infrastructure aligned with legal, ethical, and AI-specific requirements Notes: Emphasizes GDPR, data minimization, auditability

score_anchors.0: No alignment with GDPR, data minimization, or auditability.
score_anchors.1: Partial compliance; some controls or pilots in place.
score_anchors.2: Full compliance and alignment with legal, ethical, and AI-specific standards.

items.evidence_examples: Compliance reports, audit logs, policy documents
items.owners: IT Security and Data Compliance Officer

score_ranges.interpretation: Basic infrastructure, where it is limited or fragmented, with minimal AI-specific capabilities. Access is inconsistent or ad hoc. Little integration or support.
Some AI-ready infrastructure is available, with partial integration and moderate support capacity. Cloud access and training for staff are developing.
Infrastructure is comprehensive, scalable, secure, and inclusive. AI systems are fully integrated into core environments and actively maintained by trained staff.

build_action_plan.intro: Before approaching to higher level of maturity in the infrastructure AI DTP area, the critical items from 3.7.2 need to be fully implemented. For each critical item the necessary action needs to be specified to be performed (see table below). From the list of foundational actions only those will be included in particular XY university case, which are scored less than 2.

foundational_actions.action: Conduct a comprehensive infrastructure audit and identify gaps in AI-ready computing resources. Ask questions and interview all employees that have anything to do with the IT infrastructure.
foundational_actions.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services, AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

foundational_actions.action: Establish secure, managed access to cloud-based AI platforms with appropriate security controls. If possible, use your school credentials for log in to ensure high usability and simplicity.
foundational_actions.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services, AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

foundational_actions.action: Allocate and maintain a dedicated, sustainable budget line for AI/IT infrastructure with multi-year planning. Includes attending the budget meetings and planning sessions at HEI or higher levels.
Launch a basic upskilling programme to ensure at least one IT team (or defined group of staff) is trained in operating and supporting AI-specific infrastructure
foundational_actions.owners: Head of IT department / Digital Services

foundational_actions.action: Implement and monitor policies to ensure inclusive and equitable access to infrastructure for students and staff aligned with HEI rules, legal and other local and/or global laws/rules.
Align data architecture and infrastructure with legal, ethical, and AI-specific requirements (e.g. GDPR) and other rules defined by your HEI.
foundational_actions.owners: IT Security and Data Compliance Officer

Emerging.action: Conduct a comprehensive infrastructure audit to assess current state, gaps, and risks
Emerging.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Emerging.action: Identify critical pilot areas (e.g. one faculty or research lab) to deploy foundational AI computing resources
Emerging.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Emerging.action: Start small: pilot GPU- based computing in one department
Emerging.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Emerging.action: Establish a cross-functional working group (IT + AI stakeholders) to define minimal technical standards for AI-readiness
Emerging.owners: AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

Emerging.action: Allocate a dedicated budget line for initial upgrades (e.g. cloud credits, hardware purchases)
Initiate vendor consultations or join consortia to explore scalable, cost-effective infrastructure models
Establish minimum accessibility standards for digital and physical infrastructure (multi- platform access, assistive technologies, equitable access for students and staff).
Define basic data management and privacy requirements for AI use (GDPR compliance, institutional data handling policy, minimum security controls).

Established.action: Expand access to cloud platforms and AI environments across all departments
Established.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Established.action: Develop an AI integration roadmap to connect tools with core, university-wide systems
Established.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Established.action: Launch a targeted upskilling training programme for IT staff on AI infrastructure
Established.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Established.action: Design and implement accessibility policies and user onboarding processes
Pilot a self-service portal for AI computing, storage, and resource scheduling
Established.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Established.action: Introduce recurring annual budget lines for AI infrastructure upgrades, including cloud credits and maintenance.
Established.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services, Faculty and Department Heads

Established.action: Implement institution-wide data management guidelines with automated compliance checks and regular security audits.
Established.owners: IT Security & Data Compliance Officer, AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

Enhanced.action: Implement continuous monitoring and predictive maintenance for AI infrastructure components
Enhanced.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Enhanced.action: Establish a centralized governance model for AI infrastructure (usage policies, sustainability, security, and reporting)
Set up AI innovation hubs or living labs with shared infrastructure for interdisciplinary collaboration
Enhanced.owners: AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

Enhanced.action: Measure and improve the energy efficiency and environmental impact of infrastructure (green AI)
Enhanced.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

Enhanced.action: Share infrastructure best practices internally and externally, and support other institutions
Integrate long-term AI infrastructure funding into institutional financial planning, with multi-year commitments and sustainability reporting.
Establish an advanced AI infrastructure competence center with certified experts who provide training, consultation, and continuous support for faculty and researchers.

kpis.intro: The following table presents the KPIs used for the infrastructure area and target values required to achieve each maturity level. For each KPI, the corresponding owner should be specified.

items.name: Percentage of students and staff with access to AI-ready environments
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

items.name: Number of teaching, research, or administrative use cases (projects) that rely on cloud-based AI services per year
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

items.name: Infrastructure uptime (%) during critical usage periods
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

items.name: Number of courses/research projects supported by AI infrastructure
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

items.name: Average response time of IT support for AI systems (hours)
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

items.name: Cost-effectiveness/ ROI of AI infrastructure (annual review completed)
Data storage growth rate for AI projects (TB/year)
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services, AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

items.name: Number of significant security incidents related to AI infrastructure (per year)
items.owners: IT Security and Data Compliance Officer

items.name: User satisfaction with AI infrastructure (survey, %)
items.owners: Accessibility and Inclusion Officer, AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee

items.name: Number of interdisciplinary collaborations enabled by AI infrastructure
items.owners: AI Task Force / Digital Transformation Committee, Faculty and Department Heads

items.name: Carbon footprint/ energy consumption of AI infrastructure (annual report published)
items.owners: Head of IT department/ Digital Services

owners_and_roles.Chief Information Officer (CIO): Oversees the overall infrastructure strategy and ensures alignment with institutional digital transformation objectives. Responsible for budget planning, long-term sustainability, and strategic vendor engagement.
owners_and_roles.Head of IT department/ Digital Services: Leads the implementation, integration, and operational management of AI infrastructure. Coordinates technical support, procurement, and system reliability. Helps with budgeting.
owners_and_roles.AI Task Force / Digital Transformation C: Provides strategic guidance, defines priorities, and validates the infrastructure roadmap. Ensures alignment with user needs across teaching, research, and administration.
owners_and_roles.Faculty and Department Heads: Identify infrastructure needs for teaching and research. Act as liaisons between academic users and technical teams. Provide feedback on system effectiveness. Manage budgets.
owners_and_roles.Procurement and Finance Units: Manage infrastructure-related purchases, licensing, and vendor contracts. Ensure value for money, regulatory compliance, and transparent reporting.
owners_and_roles.External Providers (e.g. Cloud Vendors,: Deliver technical platforms and services. Ensure SLA adherence, security compliance, and service continuity. Collaborate on integration and support.
owners_and_roles.IT Security and Data Compliance Officer: Ensures AI infrastructure meets cybersecurity, data protection, and ethical compliance requirements. Coordinates risk assessments and incident response.
owners_and_roles.Accessibility and Inclusion Officer (if: Ensures infrastructure accommodates the needs of users with disabilities or other accessibility requirements. Advises on inclusive design and access policies.
owners_and_roles.Student and Staff Representatives: Provide user feedback, help identify usability issues, and support co-creation of infrastructure policies or usage guidelines.
